title: Prefix push to DecodeSession is not awaited, risking byte-ordering violation
description: At line 572, `(session as any).push(startingPrefix)` is called without `await`. DecodeSession.push() is async; if the underlying decoder processes pushes in submission order the un-awaited call races with the subsequent fetchTier/fetchTierWithPrefix calls. The fetch starts pushing delta bytes before the prefix bytes have been delivered, producing a mid-stream start for the decoder (garbled progressive frames or a decode error). The `(session as any)` cast hints this was added hastily.
evidence: Line 572 confirmed: `(session as any).push(startingPrefix)` with no await, immediately followed by fetchTierWithPrefix/fetchTier calls that push further bytes. DecodeSession.push() is declared async; the unordered delivery produces a corrupt codestream for the decoder.

title: Relation, CameraPose, FrameSetMember, FrameSet, BurstGroup, defaultComposeBurstFrame, getSharpnessRank, argmaxSharpness are declared twice in the same file
description: types.ts contains two full duplicate blocks: `Relation`, `FrameRole`, `CameraPose`, `FrameSetMember`, `FrameSet`, `BurstGroup`, `ComposeBurstFrame`, `defaultComposeBurstFrame`, `getSharpnessRank`, and `argmaxSharpness` all appear at lines ~119-251 and again at lines ~269-401 verbatim. TypeScript will emit 'Duplicate identifier' errors for the type aliases and runtime will only expose the last definition of each function, silently discarding the first. Any consumer importing from this file in strict mode will fail to compile.
evidence: Verified: identical Phase-8 block appears at lines 119-261 and again at lines 263-411. Both blocks export the same type names (`Relation`, `FrameRole`, `CameraPose`, `FrameSetMember`, `FrameSet`, `BurstGroup`, `defaultComposeBurstFrame`, `getSharpnessRank`, `argmaxSharpness`). TypeScript emits duplicate-identifier errors for type aliases and interfaces.

title: validateManifest does not check byteEnd > byteStart for tiers, allowing zero-length or inverted ranges
description: The tier validation loop checks field types and that tier names are valid, but does not assert `t.byteEnd > t.byteStart`. A tier with `byteEnd: 0` or `byteEnd < byteStart` passes validation and will be silently stored in the manifest cache. Callers such as `fetchTierWithPrefix` would then issue a nonsensical Range request (`bytes=0--1`), and `fetchTier` would request 0 bytes, producing an empty decode that never advances `currentTier`. A similar gap exists for `byteStart !== 0` — spec §Byte Range Semantics requires all tiers begin at byte 0.
evidence: Lines 188-189 in validateManifest confirm only typeof checks on byteStart/byteEnd. No assertField for byteEnd > byteStart or byteEnd > 0. A zero or inverted range passes validation and is cached, causing malformed HTTP Range headers downstream.

title: DC tier byteEnd is set to the event's byteOffset, but byteOffset records bytes-pushed-after-push, so DC tier byteEnd may be too late for the actual dc frame boundary
description: In `profileJxl`, `bytesPushed = end` is set *after* `await session.push(...)` and *before* `await Promise.resolve()`. The frame task captures `bytesPushed` after the yield tick. This means `byteOffset` in the recorded event is the number of bytes pushed in the chunk that triggered the dc frame — not the byte that completed the dc frame. If the final chunk of the dc tier contained extra bytes past the actual dc frame boundary, the tier's `byteEnd` will be overestimated by up to one `chunkSize`. The manifest tier's `byteEnd` is then a chunk-granular upper bound rather than the exact progression boundary.
evidence: Lines 156-162 confirm: bytesPushed is set to `end` (the chunk's end boundary) after push completes, then a microtask yield allows framesTask to read it. The recorded byteOffset is chunk-granular (up to chunkSize=4096 bytes past the actual frame boundary), producing an overestimated DC tier byteEnd. This is an inherent design consequence, not a misread.

title: autoProfile double-negation: `(opts.autoProfile ?? true) !== false` is equivalent to `opts.autoProfile !== false` and always truthy when opts.autoProfile is undefined
description: The expression `const autoProfile = (opts.autoProfile ?? true) !== false` is redundant but not incorrect per se. However it creates a subtle inconsistency: `opts.autoProfile = 0` (falsy non-false) evaluates to `autoProfile = true`, while `opts.autoProfile = false` evaluates to `false`. The simpler and documented-matching form is `opts.autoProfile !== false`. The quirk is that the variable `autoProfile` is then never used to gate behavior — only `maxActiveDecoders` is adjusted, and there is no separate branch on `autoProfile`. The `this.opts.autoProfile` stored at line 197 is `opts.autoProfile ?? true` (a different expression), which will differ from the local `autoProfile` if `opts.autoProfile` was explicitly `false` (stored as `false`) vs the local variable (also `false`). They are consistent for the two relevant values.
evidence: The expression is functionally correct for all boolean inputs: `false` maps to false, `true`/`undefined` map to true. The stored `this.opts.autoProfile` (line 197: `opts.autoProfile ?? true`) is a different expression but the only usage of the local `autoProfile` is the maxActiveDecoders guard, which behaves identically. No concrete failure scenario exists.

title: normaliseCenter does not clamp output to [0,1]: pixel coordinates beyond image dimensions produce out-of-range normalized values
description: `normaliseCenter(cx, cy, w, h)` returns `cx/w, cy/h` with no clamping. If a caller passes pixel coordinates that exceed image dimensions (e.g., cx=810 on a 800-pixel-wide image), the returned x will be 1.0125, which fails the manifest validator's `centerX in [0,1]` check and causes `validateManifest` to throw when the manifest is later stored. The function validates that dimensions are positive but not that inputs are within bounds, so the error is delayed and surfaces far from the source.
evidence: Line 55 confirmed: `{ x: cx / imageWidth, y: cy / imageHeight }` with no clamping. Out-of-bounds pixel coordinates pass through, producing values outside [0,1]. The manifest schema requires centerX/centerY in [0,1] and validateManifest would throw on an out-of-range value.

title: Dead `v < 0` branch in defaultComposeBurstFrame: Uint8Array elements are always >= 0
description: `defaultComposeBurstFrame` adds two `Uint8Array` elements and clamps: `v > 255 ? 255 : (v < 0 ? 0 : v)`. Since both operands are `Uint8Array` values (0–255), their sum is always 0–510; the `v < 0` branch is unreachable dead code. This masks potential future bugs if the type assumption ever changes (e.g., signed residuals), because the guard will be trusted but can never fire.
evidence: Lines 201-202 confirmed: `b` and `r` are both `new Uint8Array(...)`, so their elements are in [0,255]. Their sum is in [0,510]; `v < 0` is unreachable. Dead branch confirmed, though functionally harmless.

title: No-manifest fallback sets currentTier to targetTier (may skip tiers), but targetTier can be 'preview' while actual decode only completed 'dc'
description: When no manifest tier is found, `achieved = job.targetTier` (line 664) and `job.currentTier = achieved`. If the full file was fetched via `fetchFull` but the decode only produced a dc frame before budget exhaustion, `currentTier` is set to `targetTier` regardless. This means the job reports having completed up to `targetTier` even if the underlying session never reached that tier. The D-4 test treats the jump as intentional, but does not verify the actual frame output — it only checks that `currentTier` ends up at `targetTier`.
evidence: Line 664 confirmed: in the no-manifest path, `achieved = job.targetTier` (which can be 'full') while `target = nextTier(job.currentTier)` (one step up). The job's tier is advanced to `targetTier` skipping intermediate tiers, and onTier fires with the wrong tier value. A concrete failure: job at 'none' with targetTier='full' and no manifest — single fetchFull sets currentTier to 'full', firing onTier('full') with dc/preview never acknowledged.

title: No cross-tier ordering invariant: tiers array could list dc with byteEnd > preview's byteEnd
description: validateManifest checks each tier individually but not that tiers are ordered by byteEnd (dc < preview < full) and that tier names are unique. A manifest with two 'dc' tiers, or with dc.byteEnd > preview.byteEnd, passes validation and will cause `lookupTier` to return the first match while the scheduler's progressive ordering assumptions break silently. Adding cross-tier invariant checks would prevent this class of corrupted-manifest bugs from propagating into the scheduler.
evidence: validateManifest only iterates tiers one at a time with no cross-tier checks. Duplicate tier names and inverted byteEnd ordering are both accepted. `lookupTier` uses Array.find which returns the first match — duplicate 'dc' entries would always surface the first one, silently ignoring the second.

title: No property-based or fuzz tests for validateManifest boundary conditions
description: The manifest validator has numeric range checks on saliency fields (0-1) and structural checks on tiers, but all current tests use fixed valid/invalid examples. Property-based tests that generate random byteStart/byteEnd/progressionIndex combinations would catch edge cases like negative values, NaN, Infinity, or byteEnd < byteStart that the current test suite never exercises. This is particularly important because manifests arrive from untrusted network sources.
evidence: Multiple confirmed gaps in validateManifest (logic-3, security-2) that hand-crafted tests miss: negative byteEnd, inverted byteStart/byteEnd, duplicate tier names. Property-based tests would catch these automatically. Capability is genuinely missing.

title: manifestDispatched gate never reset on job retry, so onManifest never fires after first error recovery
description: When a decode attempt fails (error path), `job.manifestDispatched` is not reset. On a subsequent retry tick the manifest may already be loaded (from prior run or cache), but the `if (job.manifest && !job.manifestDispatched)` guard at line 507 fires only once per job lifetime. If the first decode attempt obtained the manifest but then failed before any frame was emitted, the consumer's `onManifest` callback will have been called. But if the manifest was not obtained in the first attempt (e.g., network failure), `manifestDispatched` remains false and the manifest dispatch will fire correctly on retry. This is subtle but correct as written — however if a race causes `job.manifest` to be set by `prefetchManifest` while `manifestDispatched` is already true, the consumer never receives `onManifest`.
evidence: The gate correctly fires once per job lifetime, which is the intended contract (onManifest should fire exactly once). The scenario where prefetchManifest sets job.manifest after manifestDispatched=true (from startDecode) is a confirmed race (see contracts-9/concurrency-1), but in that case the manifest was already dispatched, so the consumer already received it. Whether 'never fires after first error recovery' is a real failure depends on whether manifestDispatched gets set before the error — cannot determine from code alone.

title: armEarliestRetryTimer skips re-arming when a new earliest time equals the already-armed time, even if the old timer was cancelled via unobserve
description: At line 414, `if (this.armedRetryAt === earliest && this.retryTimerId !== null) return` short-circuits. However `unobserve` at line 249-252 clears `retryTimerId` and `armedRetryAt` for any job removal. After unobserve the armedRetryAt is null, so on next tick the arm fires correctly. But if `unobserve` runs *while* `armEarliestRetryTimer` is executing (impossible in single-threaded JS), or if `clearTimeoutFn` is a no-op stub (test injection), the guard may cause a missed re-arm.
evidence: JavaScript is single-threaded; unobserve cannot run concurrently with armEarliestRetryTimer. The combined guard `armedRetryAt === earliest && retryTimerId !== null` handles the only valid inconsistent state (retryTimerId null but armedRetryAt non-null) by falling through to re-arm. No production failure scenario exists.

title: prefetchManifest swallows all errors including OOM and stack overflow, losing diagnostics entirely
description: The `.catch(() => {})` on line 435 discards every thrown value without any logging or onError dispatch. A network error, JSON parse failure, or ManifestValidationError all become silent no-ops. The caller never learns why the manifest was unavailable, and the job stays with `manifestChecked: true` so no retry is ever attempted from the prefetch path. `inFlightManifestFetches` is correctly decremented in `finally`, but the failed state is permanent.
evidence: Line 435 confirmed: `.catch(() => {})` with no logging, no onError dispatch. fetchAndCacheManifest itself also returns null on any catch (errors-2), so the .catch here is a redundant silent layer. manifestChecked=true is set before the fetch, so a failed prefetch permanently blocks the prefetch path with no observable signal.

title: fetchAndCacheManifest bare catch swallows all errors including ManifestValidationError, returns null instead of propagating
description: The `catch` block on line 737 catches every exception and returns null, including ManifestValidationError (bad server data) and network failures. This prevents the caller (startDecode) from distinguishing a permanent manifest schema error from a transient network blip. As a result the scheduler may silently proceed without a manifest and skip onManifest, rather than backing off or reporting the error to onError.
evidence: Lines 737-739 confirmed: bare catch returns null unconditionally. ManifestValidationError (permanent bad-schema) and a transient 503 are indistinguishable to callers. When called from startDecode the error is swallowed entirely with no onError callback.

title: session.push() call for prefix bytes is fire-and-forget via `(session as any).push()` — rejection is never awaited or caught
description: At line 572, `(session as any).push(startingPrefix)` is called without `await`. If the underlying DecodeSession.push() returns a rejected promise (e.g. because the session was already cancelled), the rejection becomes an unhandled promise rejection, potentially crashing the Node.js process. The `(session as any)` cast also bypasses TypeScript's type checking entirely.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1/contracts-6/concurrency-3 — same line 572. Confirmed: fire-and-forget push with unhandled rejection. The same finding is captured across four detectors; all confirmed by code.

title: checkHash called with `fullPrefix` (Uint8Array) not its underlying ArrayBuffer, but the function expects ArrayBuffer
description: At line 683, `checkHash(job.manifest, fullPrefix)` passes a `Uint8Array` as the second argument. `checkHash` in progressive-manifest.ts calls `crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', jxlBytes)` which accepts `ArrayBuffer | ArrayBufferView`, so this technically works, but the Node.js fallback branch calls `Buffer.from(jxlBytes)` where `jxlBytes` is typed as `ArrayBuffer`. If `fullPrefix` is a subarray view with a non-zero `byteOffset`, `Buffer.from(fullPrefix)` will copy from the correct offset and length, but there is a type mismatch between what the function signature declares (`ArrayBuffer`) and what is passed (`Uint8Array`). More critically, the buffer passed to `checkHash` is the captured prefix bytes only (from byte 0 to `prefixBytes`), but the manifest `jxl.sha256` was computed over the full original file. So hash verification on a non-full tier will always fail — the hash check is architecturally only meaningful for the 'full' tier, and it IS gated on `achieved === 'full'`, but `fullPrefix` contains only the captured prefix which may not equal the full file if the server returned fewer bytes than `byteEnd` due to the `settled()` race.
code_excerpt: const ok = await checkHash(job.manifest, fullPrefix);
evidence: The check is correctly gated on `achieved === 'full'` (line 682). `fullPrefix` is produced by `job.prefixAccum.slice(0, job.prefixBytes)` which always creates a Uint8Array with byteOffset=0, so Buffer.from(fullPrefix) in Node.js correctly reads the full slice. The type mismatch is a TypeScript annotation issue (ArrayBuffer vs ArrayBufferView) but not a runtime failure. The 'fullPrefix may not be the full file' scenario requires the settled() race to lose bytes, which is a separate issue — not a bug in this call itself.

title: `void this.startDecode(job)` discards the returned promise — if startDecode throws outside its own try/catch it becomes an unhandled rejection
description: The `void` operator on line 483 discards the promise. The `startDecode` method has a comprehensive `try/catch/finally`, but the catch block itself calls `this.opts.onError` which is user-supplied and could throw. Any exception escaping the catch or finally block would become an unhandled rejection with no recovery path.
evidence: Line 483 confirmed: `void this.startDecode(job)` with no .catch. The startDecode finally block calls `this.requestTick()` which could throw if destroyed improperly; the catch block calls `this.opts.onError` which is user-supplied. Any exception escaping both becomes an unhandled rejection in Node.js (process crash) or browser (unhandledrejection event).

title: selectTiers uses totalBytes=0 comparisons without guarding against zero-length input, and can produce a dc tier with byteEnd=0
description: If `jxlBytes.byteLength === 0` is passed to `profileJxl`, `totalBytes` will be 0. `events.find(e => e.byteOffset < totalBytes * 0.25)` will match no events (all byteOffsets are >= 0), but `events[0]` could still be used as `dcEvent`. Then `tiers.push({ byteEnd: dcEvent.byteOffset })` would produce a dc tier with `byteEnd: 0`.
evidence: profileJxl with zero-length input: the pushTask loop at line 153 `while (offset < total)` skips entirely (0 < 0 is false), session.close() is called, framesTask gets no events, events=[], selectTiers([],0) returns only a single 'full' tier with byteEnd=0. A zero-length full tier bypasses the byteEnd=0 guard in getByteRange but is silently stored. The hang scenario (framesTask awaiting on a never-closed session) does not occur because the session.close() call correctly ends the stream.

title: fetchAndCacheManifest has no timeout — a stalled manifest fetch holds `inFlightManifestFetches` slot indefinitely
description: The `fetch(job.manifestUrl)` call has no timeout signal. If the server accepts the TCP connection but never responds (hung request), `inFlightManifestFetches` will remain incremented indefinitely. Since `prefetchManifest` guards on `this.inFlightManifestFetches < this.opts.maxConcurrentFetches`, all slots can be consumed by stalled manifest fetches, permanently blocking new near-viewport manifest prefetches without any user-visible error.
evidence: Lines 731-733 confirmed: no AbortSignal/timeout on fetch. The inFlightManifestFetches counter at line 430 is incremented before the async fetch and only decremented in the finally block (line 437). A permanently stalled TCP connection holds the slot forever, blocking the maxConcurrentFetches (default 3) guard.

title: RangeNotSupportedError fallback in teeFetch swallows all errors from the second ft() call, not just RangeNotSupportedError
description: In the fallback branch at line 630-634, if `ft(...)` (plain fetchTier) throws any error (e.g. HttpError 404, network error), it is silently assigned to `fetchError` but the outer `catch (e)` on line 621 has already been consumed. The `fetchError` variable is eventually rethrown only if `!abort.signal.aborted`, but if the session was aborted during the fallback fetch, `fetchError` is silently dropped. This means a permanent 404 on a fallback full-range fetch is not reflected in `job.nextRetryAt = Infinity`.
evidence: Lines 629-634 confirmed: fallback ft() errors are stored in `fetchError` via .catch, but if abort fires before the post-frames error check at line 659, fetchError is dropped silently. A 404 on the fallback path never triggers `job.nextRetryAt = Infinity`, causing repeated retries for a permanently absent resource.

title: getManifest bare catch swallows all decode/parse errors including OOM, returns null silently
description: The `catch { return null; }` on lines 66-68 swallows TextDecoder failures, JSON.parse errors, ManifestValidationError from validateManifest, and any error thrown by `this.inner.set()` in the expiry branch. Callers cannot distinguish a corrupted cache entry from a missing one. This is defensive but eliminates observability — a persistently corrupt entry (e.g. truncated write from prior crash) will silently serve null on every cache hit forever.
evidence: Lines 66-68 confirmed: bare `catch { return null; }` with no logging. A corrupt OPFS entry produced by a prior crash will silently return null on every access; the cache never self-heals because the entry is not evicted on parse failure.

title: Error catch block uses `performance.now()` for retry timestamp but `nextRetryAt` is compared against `Date.now()` in tick(), causing permanent retry suppression on some platforms
description: In the catch block (line 706), `now` is computed as `performance.now()` (milliseconds since page load, origin ~0). In `tick()` (line 468), `now` is also `performance.now()`. So far consistent. However, if the branch on line 469 where `performance` is undefined falls through to `Date.now()` (e.g. in a Node test environment), then `nextRetryAt` set in catch uses `performance.now()` but the tick comparison uses `Date.now()`. Since `performance.now()` values (e.g. 5000 ms since page load) are far less than `Date.now()` (e.g. 1750000000000 epoch ms), `now >= job.nextRetryAt` will always be true and the backoff is never observed.
evidence: Both the catch block (line 706) and tick() (line 469) use the identical expression `typeof performance !== 'undefined' ? performance.now() : Date.now()`. They are always in sync: if performance is defined both use performance.now(); if not, both use Date.now(). No mixed-clock scenario is possible at runtime.

title: Exponential backoff `1000 * 2 ** job.errorCount` overflows to Infinity for errorCount >= 1024
description: JavaScript's `2 ** 1024` evaluates to `Infinity`, making `Math.min(Infinity, 30_000)` equal to `30_000`, so this is actually safe for large errorCounts due to Math.min capping. However, for `errorCount` around 31-53, `2**errorCount` overflows the safe integer range, and for `errorCount >= 1074`, it becomes `Infinity`. The `Math.min(..., 30_000)` cap saves the final value, but the intermediate `1000 * 2**31` (~2 trillion) is a Number that still compares correctly so the overflow is benign in practice.
code_excerpt: job.nextRetryAt = now + Math.min(1000 * 2 ** job.errorCount, 30_000);
evidence: Math.min(1000 * 2**errorCount, 30_000) correctly caps at 30_000 for all errorCount values including Infinity cases (Math.min(Infinity, 30_000) === 30_000). The intermediate large Number values are representable as IEEE-754 doubles and compare correctly. No concrete failure scenario.

title: pushTask and framesTask run in parallel but a push() rejection only rejects pushTask — framesTask continues iterating a dead session causing a hang
description: At line 167, `Promise.all([pushTask, framesTask])` is used. If `session.push()` rejects (e.g. the session errors mid-stream), `pushTask` will reject and `Promise.all` will short-circuit. However, `framesTask` continues its `for await (const frame of session.frames())` loop. If the session does not terminate the frames() generator on push failure, `framesTask` will hang forever. `Promise.all` rejection does not cancel the sibling task.
code_excerpt: await Promise.all([pushTask, framesTask]);
evidence: Lines 140-167 confirmed: framesTask iterates `session.frames()` async generator independently of pushTask. Promise.all rejects when pushTask rejects but does not cancel framesTask. If the session implementation does not close its frames() generator on error, framesTask hangs indefinitely holding the profileJxl caller in a permanently unresolved state.

title: No error taxonomy / sentinel error types — callers cannot distinguish network, abort, validation, or hash errors programmatically
description: The `onError` callback receives a generic `Error` for all failure modes: network errors, ManifestValidationError, hash verification failures, and HttpError are all passed through. Callers must parse `e.message` strings to distinguish permanent from transient errors.
evidence: Line 714 confirmed: onError receives a generic Error. HttpError (already a typed subclass) is passed through but ManifestValidationError and hash errors are wrapped in generic Error at line 689. Callers must inspect message strings. Capability genuinely missing — no ProgressiveLoadError type or kind discriminant exists in the codebase.

title: No default fetch timeout utility — all fetch calls (manifest + tier + full) are unbounded without caller-supplied AbortSignal timeout
description: Neither `fetchAndCacheManifest` nor `fetchTier`/`fetchFull`/`fetchTierWithPrefix` impose a default timeout on network operations. A hung TCP connection will hold decoder slots and manifest-fetch slots indefinitely.
code_excerpt: const resp = await fetch(job.manifestUrl); // no timeout
evidence: Confirmed by errors-7 (manifest fetch) and code review of fetch calls at lines 618, 631, 641, 647 — none have timeout signals. GalleryOptions has no fetchTimeoutMs field. Capability genuinely absent.

category: cache key collision / incorrect data access
title: Cache key separator '#' in jxlUrl causes key collision between manifest/byte-range namespaces and wrong eviction in evictBitmaps
description: Byte-range keys are built as `BYTES_KEY_PREFIX + jxlUrl + '#' + tier`. If a caller passes a jxlUrl that contains a '#' character (e.g. a URL with a fragment, or a data URL), `key.lastIndexOf('#')` in `evictBitmaps` (line 139) strips into the URL part rather than the tier suffix, producing a wrong `urlPart` that never matches `keep` entries and evicts bitmaps that should be retained. Additionally, a jxlUrl ending in '#dc', '#preview', or '#full' could produce a byte-range key identical to a different URL's tier key, silently serving the wrong bytes. The `invalidate` method (line 159) uses `key.includes(jxlUrl)` which is also susceptible: a short URL that is a substring of a longer URL causes unintended cross-URL eviction.
evidence: Lines 97 and 139 confirmed: key scheme uses '#' as tier separator without encoding jxlUrl, and evictBitmaps parses via lastIndexOf('#'). A jxlUrl containing '#' will cause incorrect urlPart extraction. invalidate() at line 160 uses `key.includes(jxlUrl)` which fails for substring URLs.

title: validateManifest does not reject negative, non-finite, or inverted byteStart/byteEnd values in tier entries
description: The tier validation checks only that `byteStart` and `byteEnd` are numbers (typeof check), but does not verify they are finite, non-negative, or that byteStart <= byteEnd. A malicious or malformed manifest with `byteEnd: -1` or `byteStart: Infinity` passes validation and is stored in the cache. Downstream `fetchTierWithPrefix` builds an HTTP Range header as `bytes=${prefixLength}-${tier.byteEnd - 1}` (progressive-stream.ts:115); a negative byteEnd produces `bytes=0--2` which is an invalid Range header.
code_excerpt:
assertField(typeof t["byteStart"] === "number", `${f}.byteStart`, `${f}.byteStart must be a number`);
assertField(typeof t["byteEnd"] === "number", `${f}.byteEnd`, `${f}.byteEnd must be a number`);
evidence: Same code path as logic-3/contracts-3. Lines 188-189: only typeof checks. NaN, Infinity, negative values, and inverted ranges all pass validation. Confirmed independently by multiple detectors.

category: unbounded resource consumption / DoS via storage amplification
title: No length bounds on encoder.name, encoder.libjxlVersion, encoder.flags array, or perceptual passthrough — malicious manifest can cause unbounded cache storage
description: validateManifest accepts arbitrarily long strings for `encoder.name`, `encoder.libjxlVersion`, and each element of `encoder.flags`, and accepts an arbitrarily deep/wide `perceptual` object. The manifest is serialised via JSON.stringify and stored in the persistent OPFS cache. A remote server can serve a crafted manifest with megabyte-sized strings in these fields, causing unbounded writes to the origin's OPFS storage quota.
code_excerpt:
assertField(typeof enc["name"] === "string", "encoder.name", "encoder.name must be a string");
assertField(typeof enc["libjxlVersion"] === "string", "encoder.libjxlVersion", ...);
evidence: validateManifest checks typeof string for encoder.name/libjxlVersion but no length bound. perceptual is accepted as any object without depth or size limit. Manifests are stored to OPFS via setManifest (progressive-cache.ts:80) with no byte-length cap. A crafted manifest can exhaust origin storage quota.

title: Hand-rolled manifest validation has no numeric-range checks and no maximum-depth guard for nested objects — a Zod/valibot schema would close all current and future gaps at once
description: The current `validateManifest` performs only type-of checks and misses several classes of invalid input: non-finite numbers, out-of-range values, unbounded string lengths, and deeply nested objects that could cause stack overflow during JSON.parse or recursive validation. A Zod or valibot schema would enforce these constraints in one declaration.
code_excerpt: // perceptual passthrough (optional, loose for future color science transport)
evidence: Multiple confirmed gaps in validateManifest (logic-3, security-2, security-3, contracts-3, contracts-14) all stem from the same hand-rolled approach. Capability genuinely missing — no schema library is used anywhere in the package.

title: prefetchManifest guard races with concurrent startDecode: two fetches may inflate inFlightManifestFetches simultaneously
description: prefetchManifest sets job.manifestChecked = true and increments inFlightManifestFetches synchronously before the async fetch, but startDecode (line 503) also independently fetches the manifest without checking or incrementing inFlightManifestFetches. If the IntersectionObserver fires prefetchManifest while a tick concurrently dispatches startDecode for the same job, both code paths run concurrently: prefetchManifest's .then() at line 432 may overwrite job.manifest after startDecode has already set it (no conflict in itself), but inFlightManifestFetches is not decremented in the startDecode path, making the counter a lower bound only.
evidence: Lines 428-430: prefetchManifest guards on manifestChecked and increments inFlightManifestFetches. Lines 503-505: startDecode also calls fetchAndCacheManifest but does NOT increment inFlightManifestFetches and only checks manifestChecked (set by prefetch). If IntersectionObserver fires prefetchManifest synchronously before a tick runs, inFlightManifestFetches is incremented but not by startDecode, making the counter incorrect. The maxConcurrentFetches guard is only enforced in prefetchManifest.

title: tick() increments activeDecoders before await but startDecode is void-dispatched; a second tick fires before the first startDecode returns, allowing activeDecoders to be exceeded
description: tick() calls `void this.startDecode(job)` at line 483 — a fire-and-forget dispatch. startDecode increments activeDecoders at line 487 synchronously as the first line of its body, but the tick() loop checks `this.activeDecoders >= this.opts.maxActiveDecoders` before calling startDecode. Because `void` does not await, the loop runs all eligible candidates before any startDecode has a chance to increment the counter; however each call to startDecode is itself an async function starting synchronously, so the increment at line 487 DOES execute before the loop's next iteration.
evidence: An async function runs synchronously up to its first await. startDecode's first line (487) increments activeDecoders before any await, so the tick() loop sees the updated count on the very next iteration. Within a single tick() call, maxActiveDecoders is correctly enforced. The cross-RAF-frame concern requires requestTick to be called and RAF to fire while startDecode is still active, but decoderAbort (set synchronously at line 491) prevents the job from re-entering the candidates filter.

title: session.push() called fire-and-forget with (session as any).push() — unawaited async call during prefix seeding
description: At line 572, the prefix is pushed into the session with `(session as any).push(startingPrefix)` — the return value (a Promise) is not awaited. If the session's push() is async (as per DecodeSession contract), the delta fetch begins before push() completes. This means the JXL decoder state machine may not have processed the prefix bytes when the delta fetch starts pushing subsequent bytes, producing an out-of-order codestream delivered to the decoder.
evidence: Same as logic-1/errors-3/contracts-6. Confirmed across all four detectors. Line 572: fire-and-forget push immediately followed by fetchTier/fetchTierWithPrefix stream pushes, producing out-of-order delivery to the JXL decoder.

title: void this.inner.set() in getManifest expiry path — eviction write is fire-and-forget
description: When a manifest is detected as expired (line 60), the code calls `void this.inner.set(key, new ArrayBuffer(0))` to evict it and immediately returns null. The eviction write is fire-and-forget: if a concurrent getManifest call races before the write completes, it will parse the same expired entry and return it again.
evidence: Line 62 confirmed: `void this.inner.set(...)` fire-and-forget. A concurrent getManifest call (e.g. from startDecode and a prefetch retry running on the same job) can both read the expired entry and both return the stale manifest before the eviction write completes.

title: Manifest fetch inside startDecode not guarded by AbortSignal: leaks a network request on early abort
description: startDecode creates abort at line 490 and stores it on job.decoderAbort, but the manifest fetch calls (line 501 via `this.cache.getManifest` and line 504 via `this.fetchAndCacheManifest`) are not passed the abort signal. If destroy() or unobserve() aborts the controller between line 487 (activeDecoders++) and line 501, the manifest network request is already in flight with no cancellation path.
evidence: Lines 500-505 confirmed: neither cache.getManifest nor fetchAndCacheManifest receive the abort signal. fetchAndCacheManifest (lines 730-740) calls fetch() without any signal. The abort is only threaded into tier/full fetches starting at lines 618-651.

title: Bitmap fast path reads cache then mutates job.currentTier without rechecking abort signal
description: The bitmap cache fast path (lines 529-541) awaits `this.cache.getBitmap()`, then immediately sets job.currentTier and fires onTier. But between the getBitmap await and the currentTier mutation, the AbortController may have fired (e.g. destroy() called or viewport exit timer fired). The abort.signal.aborted check at line 663 is only after the frame streaming loop, not inside the bitmap fast path's early-return.
evidence: The bitmap fast path at lines 529-541 lacks an abort check after the await. An abort between the getBitmap await and the currentTier mutation fires onTier on an already-destroyed or unobserved job, advancing tier state on a job that may have been removed from this.jobs.

title: framesTask and pushTask share mutable bytesPushed with only a microtask-yield for ordering — ordering depends on event loop behaviour
description: profileJxl intentionally shares the mutable `bytesPushed` variable between pushTask and framesTask (line 135). The comment on line 158 states 'Yield a microtask tick so frame events triggered by this push can be picked up by the frames task with the correct bytesPushed.' If the session implementation delivers frames synchronously (i.e. emits them inside push() before push() returns), the framesTask is not yet awaiting and will read the wrong (previous) bytesPushed value.
evidence: Lines 156-160 confirmed: bytesPushed is updated after push(), then Promise.resolve() yields a microtask. A session that emits frames synchronously inside push() before push() resolves would queue frame events with the pre-push bytesPushed value (read when framesTask was last awaiting), causing incorrect byteOffset in the tier map. This is a latent bug conditional on session implementation.

title: teeFetch pump promise swallows errors silently; toCapture ReadableStream reader is not released on abort
description: The teeFetch() method creates a tee of the response body and starts draining toCapture in a background pump. The pump catches all errors silently (.catch(() => {})). If abort fires while the pump is consuming toCapture, the reader is never explicitly cancelled or released. More critically, the pump promise stored in `pump` is replaced on fallback (line 629): the original pump is abandoned with its reader still open, leaking the stream consumer.
evidence: Lines 445-463: teeFetch creates a pump that holds a ReadableStreamDefaultReader. On RangeNotSupportedError fallback (line 629), `capture = t2` replaces the reference but the original pump continues draining toCapture with no cancellation. The reader from the abandoned pump is never released via r.cancel(), leaking the stream consumer for the duration of the original response body.

title: job.prefixAccum mutated post-await in finally-adjacent success path without rechecking abort, allowing cross-decode prefix corruption on fast re-queue
description: After the frame loop and fetchP await complete (line 658), the code checks abort.signal.aborted (line 663) before advancing job.currentTier. However the subsequent prefix persist block (lines 671-700) also writes job.prefixAccum and job.prefixBytes. If destroy() or unobserve() is called between the abort check and the persist block, the writes happen on a potentially stale job reference. Since unobserve clears prefixAccum, re-observe creates a fresh job object, so the write goes to the detached object — harmless.
evidence: unobserve() removes the job from this.jobs and clears prefixAccum on the removed object. A subsequent observe() with the same id creates a new job object. Since `job` is a local reference in startDecode, writes after unobserve go to the detached (now-unreferenced except by this function) job object — not the new job. No state corruption is possible.

title: fetchAndCacheManifest has no AbortSignal parameter — no way to cancel in-flight manifest fetch
description: fetchAndCacheManifest() calls the global fetch() with no signal, so manifest fetches cannot be cancelled when the gallery is destroyed or a job is unobserved. Adding an optional signal parameter and threading it through would allow callers (startDecode, prefetchManifest) to cancel these fetches on abort.
evidence: Lines 727-740 confirmed: no AbortSignal parameter. This is a broader framing of concurrency-5 — the root cause is that fetchAndCacheManifest itself never accepts a signal. Capability genuinely missing.

title: Entire Phase-8 type block is duplicated verbatim at lines 263–411, causing two identical export declarations in the same file
description: Lines 119–207 define and export Relation, FrameRole, CameraPose, FrameSetMember, FrameSet, BurstGroup, ComposeBurstFrame, defaultComposeBurstFrame. Lines 263–357 copy the exact same interfaces and functions a second time under the same export names. TypeScript will surface a 'Duplicate identifier' error for each name.
evidence: Same as logic-2. Verified by direct inspection: lines 119-261 and 263-411 are verbatim duplicates of the same Phase-8 block. Two export declarations for the same name in one TS module causes duplicate identifier errors.

title: selectTiers may return tiers with byteEnd === 0 when dcEvent.byteOffset is 0, violating the manifest contract that byteEnd > 0 for a usable tier
description: In selectTiers, the dc tier is assigned byteEnd = dcEvent.byteOffset (line 72). If the session fires a frame at byteOffset 0 (possible when bytesPushed is read before the first push completes due to the Promise.resolve() microtask yield), dcEvent.byteOffset is 0. The resulting ManifestTier has byteEnd = 0. validateManifest does not range-check byteEnd > 0 and will accept the zero value.
code_excerpt: byteEnd: dcEvent.byteOffset,  // can be 0
evidence: Line 71: `byteEnd: dcEvent.byteOffset`. The framesTask captures bytesPushed at time of frame emission; bytesPushed is initialized to 0 and only set to `end` after push(). If the session fires a frame synchronously inside the first push(), framesTask (which reads bytesPushed before the yield) would record byteOffset=0. The resulting dc tier with byteEnd=0 is stored in the manifest.

title: validateManifest does not check byteStart <= byteEnd or byteEnd > 0 on any tier, so invalid range values pass validation and produce unfetchable or undefined-behaviour tiers
description: The manifest schema (ManifestTier) carries byteStart and byteEnd as raw numbers. validateManifest verifies their types but not their values: byteEnd could be 0, negative, or less than byteStart.
evidence: Same finding as logic-3/security-2. Confirmed by code inspection — no assertField for range checks exists in validateManifest.

title: When no manifestTier exists (fallback fetchFull path), job.currentTier is set to job.targetTier rather than the literal tier that was fetched, allowing a jump of multiple tiers in one step
description: At line 664: const achieved = manifestTier !== undefined ? target : job.targetTier. In the no-manifest case, achieved = job.targetTier which may be 'full' while currentTier is 'none' — a two-step jump. This means onTier fires with 'full' while 'dc' and 'preview' were never acknowledged.
code_excerpt: const achieved = manifestTier !== undefined ? target : job.targetTier;
evidence: Same as logic-8. Confirmed at line 664. In the no-manifest path, `target = nextTier(job.currentTier)` (one tier up) but `achieved = job.targetTier` (potentially 'full'). onTier fires with the wrong value, breaking consumers that gate rendering on tier sequence.

title: ProgressiveGallery.observe() silently drops the call when jobs.size >= maxQueuedJobs with no notification to the caller, leaking the expectation that the image will be displayed
description: When the gallery is at capacity (line 212: if (this.jobs.size >= this.opts.maxQueuedJobs) return), observe() returns void without any indication of failure. The caller's element is not registered with IntersectionObserver and no frames or tier callbacks will ever fire for it. There is no onError call, no thrown exception, and no returned status.
evidence: Line 212 confirmed: silent return when at capacity. No onError dispatch, no return value, no exception. A UI component that calls observe() and awaits at least one callback for visible elements will wait indefinitely.

title: session.push(startingPrefix) is called with (session as any).push(), bypassing the DecodeSession type and ignoring the returned Promise
description: Line 572: (session as any).push(startingPrefix). The cast to any circumvents TypeScript's type safety and the Promise returned by push() is not awaited. If push() throws or rejects, the error is silently lost and the decode continues as if the prefix was fed successfully.
evidence: Same as logic-1/errors-3/concurrency-3. Confirmed — fire-and-forget with any-cast.

title: getManifest writes an empty ArrayBuffer as a 'sentinel for eviction' but the underlying JxlCacheBrowser.set() contract does not guarantee that a zero-length value is evicted
description: Line 62: void this.inner.set(key, new ArrayBuffer(0)). The comment says 'jxl-cache LRU will drop it' but the JxlCacheBrowser interface has no such contract. If the LRU does not evict zero-length entries (e.g. because eviction is size-based and 0 counts as the cheapest entry to keep), a subsequent getManifest() will find the empty buffer, fall into the parse-failure catch, and return null — which is functionally correct but masks the stale-entry condition.
code_excerpt: void this.inner.set(key, new ArrayBuffer(0)); // empty = sentinel for eviction; jxl-cache LRU will drop it
evidence: Lines 56 and 62 confirm the empty-buffer sentinel pattern. The getManifest check `if (buf === undefined || buf.byteLength === 0) return null` at line 56 short-circuits on zero-length, so the contract works functionally. However invalidate() also uses this sentinel (progressive-cache.ts:154-157), and setManifest immediately after invalidate() could store over the sentinel before eviction occurs. The LRU contract assumption is unverified.

title: toSaliency returns a Saliency with confidence omitted when opts.confidence is undefined, but ProgressiveManifest.saliency declares confidence as a required number
description: The Saliency interface declares confidence as optional (confidence?: number), but ProgressiveManifest.saliency (progressive-manifest.ts line 47) declares confidence: number (required). toSaliency conditionally omits confidence when opts.confidence is undefined (line 126). A caller passing `toSaliency(bestCenter)` without confidence gets a saliency object missing confidence, which fails validateManifest's confidence type check at retrieval time.
code_excerpt: ...(opts.confidence !== undefined ? { confidence: opts.confidence } : {}),
evidence: progressive-manifest.ts line 47: `confidence: number` (required, no ?). saliency-policy.ts line 126: confidence is only included when opts.confidence !== undefined. A manifest produced by profileJxl with toSaliency(center) and no confidence will fail validateManifest on the `typeof confidence === 'number'` check when retrieved from cache.

title: prefetchManifest races with startDecode: both paths call fetchAndCacheManifest independently; prefetch result may arrive after manifestDispatched is already true
description: prefetchManifest (line 427) fires fetchAndCacheManifest and sets job.manifest via .then(). Concurrently, startDecode sees manifestChecked=true and skips its own fetch. If the prefetch completes after startDecode has already set manifestDispatched=true (from a prior successful decode), the newly-arrived manifest is stored in job.manifest but onManifest never fires.
evidence: The .then handler at line 432-433 sets job.manifest but does not dispatch onManifest. onManifest is dispatched only inside startDecode (lines 507-510) gated on !manifestDispatched. If prefetch arrives after startDecode has set manifestDispatched=true, the manifest is stored but onManifest never fires for it.

category: enum not handled by all consumers
title: nextTier returns null for 'full' but callers can set targetTier below currentTier causing permanent silent exclusion
description: nextTier(current: Tier): Tier | null returns null only for 'full'. In startDecode, line 521: const target = nextTier(job.currentTier); if (target === null) return. The return is inside startDecode after activeDecoders has already been incremented (line 487). The finally block decrements it, so the count stays correct. However tick() at line 472-473 filters candidates with tierRank(j.currentTier) < tierRank(j.targetTier), which should prevent full-tier jobs from entering the queue. The gap is: setTargetTier(id, 'none') is not prevented at the public API level.
evidence: The filter at line 473 (`tierRank(j.currentTier) < tierRank(j.targetTier)`) prevents any job where targetTier <= currentTier from being scheduled. A setTargetTier-style call that lowers targetTier below currentTier would cause permanent silent exclusion. However the public API does not expose setTargetTier — cannot confirm without knowing the full API surface.

title: profileJxlFile writes the manifest by default with undocumented writeManifest flag — side effect not in exported type
description: profileJxlFile is exported as a public API. The `writeManifest?: boolean` extension is not part of ProfileOptions. The write behavior is default-on (line 208: if (opts.writeManifest !== false)), meaning callers who pass no writeManifest option will have a .json file silently written beside their .jxl file. This side effect is undocumented in the exported type.
evidence: Lines 199 and 208 confirmed: writeManifest is not in the exported ProfileOptions type (it's appended inline), and the default behavior is to write the file. A caller importing ProfileOptions sees no indication of the filesystem side effect.

title: evictBitmaps prefix-strips jxlUrl from bitmap keys using lastIndexOf('#') — fails for jxlUrls containing '#'
description: evictBitmaps builds keep as a Set of BITMAP_KEY_PREFIX + jxlUrl (no tier suffix). For each key it strips from the last '#' to get urlPart. If jxlUrl contains a '#' fragment, urlPart will be truncated at that '#' and not match the keep set. The eviction will incorrectly delete bitmaps for URLs that should be kept.
evidence: Same issue as security-1 for the bitmap store path. Line 139 confirmed: lastIndexOf('#') strips to the last '#' in the full key, which is the tier separator. But if jxlUrl itself contains '#', the tier is appended after it and the last '#' is the tier one — actually correct in that case. However the keep set uses BITMAP_KEY_PREFIX + jxlUrl (without '#tier'), so it won't match any key anyway. The mismatch is that keep prefixes lack the '#tier' portion but urlPart strips the '#tier' suffix correctly. The real bug is for jxlUrls with '#': key= prefix+url#fragment#tier, urlPart=prefix+url#fragment, keep has prefix+url#fragment — these DO match. The bug is actually for short URLs that are substrings, surfaced in invalidate() at line 160.

title: makeJob fixture includes prefixChunks: [] which is not a field in the ProgressiveImageJob interface
description: The ProgressiveImageJob interface (progressive-scheduler.ts lines 24-54) defines prefixAccum: Uint8Array | null and prefixBytes: number. There is no prefixChunks field. The test makeJob (line 123) includes prefixChunks: [] — an obsolete field from a prior design. Several tests also set job.prefixChunks = [] directly. Since the field does not exist on the interface, these assignments are dead code.
evidence: ProgressiveImageJob interface (lines 24-54) confirmed: no prefixChunks field. makeJob at line 122 sets `prefixChunks: []`. This field is accepted via TypeScript structural spread into the test fixture but is dead code — no production path reads it. Tests relying on prefixChunks to seed state are not actually exercising the real code path.

title: validateManifest does not validate the capture, channels, or channelDescriptors optional fields introduced for Phase 8
description: ProgressiveManifest now includes three optional Phase-8 fields: capture, channels, and channelDescriptors. validateManifest performs no checks on any of these fields. A manifest with a malformed capture.pose.lat = 'hello' or channels = [42] passes validation and is cached and dispatched.
evidence: validateManifest (lines 99-198) confirmed: the function validates version, source, jxl, encoder, saliency, and tiers fields but has no validation for capture, channels, or channelDescriptors. These Phase-8 fields are cast through `as ProgressiveManifest` at line 198. Capability genuinely missing.

title: migrateManifest throws on version > 1 but has no migration path; there is no version negotiation protocol for clients
description: migrateManifest currently throws ManifestValidationError for any version > 1. There is no capability negotiation: a client that fetches a v2 manifest will throw rather than degrade gracefully. The public API exposes no mechanism to query 'what manifest versions can this client handle'.
evidence: Lines 236-240 confirmed: hard throw for version > 1 with no fallback. No SUPPORTED_MANIFEST_VERSIONS export exists. Phase-8 fields are landing which may require a version bump — any v2 manifest from a newer server causes all clients running this code to hard-fail with ManifestValidationError.

title: tick() spreads entire jobs Map into an array on every RAF frame
description: Every tick() call does `[...this.jobs.values()]` which allocates a new array of all registered jobs, then chains .filter() x3 and .map() x2. With maxQueuedJobs up to 50 this happens on every animation frame (60fps) even when nothing is eligible.
code_excerpt:
const candidates = [...this.jobs.values()]
      .filter((j) => j.visible || j.nearViewport || j.selected)
      .filter(...)
      ...
evidence: Lines 471-478 confirmed: spread + 3 filters + 2 maps on every tick(). Each produces a new intermediate array. At 60fps with 50 jobs, this is ~300 short-lived allocations per second in the hot animation path.

title: tierRank() called 4 times per job per tick (two filters × two jobs in comparison)
description: Each filter pass calls `tierRank(j.currentTier)` and `tierRank(j.targetTier)` per job. tierRank performs a object-literal lookup each call. With 50 jobs this is 200 redundant lookups per tick.
code_excerpt: .filter((j) => tierRank(j.currentTier) < tierRank(j.targetTier))
evidence: Lines 473 and 101 confirmed: tierRank creates a new `{ none: 0, dc: 1, preview: 2, full: 3 }` object literal on every call and looks up the key. Called twice per job in the filter (currentTier + targetTier) and again inside fairnessScore (line 101). Redundant object creation on every tick.

title: armEarliestRetryTimer iterates all jobs on every tick even when no jobs are retrying
description: armEarliestRetryTimer is called unconditionally at the start of every tick(). It iterates the entire jobs Map to find the minimum nextRetryAt. When no jobs are in error state (the common case), this is a pure waste.
evidence: Line 470 confirmed: armEarliestRetryTimer is the first call in every tick(). Lines 399-404 confirmed: it iterates all jobs unconditionally. No early-exit when errorCount is 0 for all jobs.

title: onChunk allocates a new Uint8Array wrapper for every incoming fetch chunk
description: The onChunk capture callback does `const chunk = new Uint8Array(c)` on every chunk received from the tee stream, wrapping a Uint8Array that is already a Uint8Array. This is a redundant copy allocation.
code_excerpt:
const onChunk = (c: Uint8Array) => {
        const chunk = new Uint8Array(c);
evidence: Line 585 confirmed: `const chunk = new Uint8Array(c)` where `c` is already a Uint8Array. `new Uint8Array(existingUint8Array)` copies the data into a new backing buffer — a full allocation and memcopy for every network chunk.

title: New TextDecoder instance allocated on every getManifest cache hit
description: getManifest creates `new TextDecoder()` on every call. A module-level singleton would eliminate repeated object construction.
code_excerpt: const text = new TextDecoder().decode(buf);
evidence: Line 58 confirmed: `new TextDecoder()` on every getManifest call. Called on every tier upgrade and prefetch for every gallery image. A module-level `const UTF8_DECODER = new TextDecoder()` is a free optimization.

title: checkHash converts SHA-256 digest to hex via Array.from + map + join — allocates three intermediate collections
description: Both checkHash (progressive-manifest.ts:218-223) and computeSha256 (progressive-profile.ts:34-38) independently build the hex string by calling `Array.from(new Uint8Array(hashBuf))` which allocates a 32-element JS array, then `.map()` which allocates another 32-element array of strings, then `.join('')`.
evidence: Lines 219-221 confirmed. Also verified in progressive-profile.ts computeSha256 (same pattern). Three intermediate allocations (Uint8Array view, mapped string array, joined string) for a 32-byte result.

title: fullPrefix slice + buffer slice allocates two extra copies of the tier bytes at persist time
description: After capture.settled(), the persist path does `job.prefixAccum.slice(0, job.prefixBytes)` which allocates a new Uint8Array, then `fullPrefix.buffer.slice(fullPrefix.byteOffset, ...)` which allocates a new ArrayBuffer. For large tiers this is a costly duplicate allocation.
evidence: Lines 674-679 confirmed. `.slice()` on a Uint8Array always creates a new copy (unlike `.subarray()`). Then `.buffer.slice()` creates another ArrayBuffer copy. For preview/full tiers this can be megabytes duplicated in memory. Using subarray + buffer.slice with correct offsets (or just buffer.slice directly) would eliminate one copy.

title: evictBitmaps rebuilds a Set of keep-prefixes on every call
description: evictBitmaps maps the exceptJxlUrls array to full prefixed keys with `new Set(exceptJxlUrls.map(u => BITMAP_KEY_PREFIX + u))` on every invocation.
evidence: Lines 133-136 confirmed: new Set + map on every call. Minor but confirmed.

title: invalidate() iterates bitmapStore via spread of keys() into a temporary array
description: `invalidate` does `[...this.bitmapStore.keys()]` to copy all keys before iterating. Map.prototype.keys() is safe to iterate while deleting entries that are visited later — the spread is unnecessary.
evidence: Line 159 confirmed: `[...this.bitmapStore.keys()]` spread is unnecessary. ES spec guarantees that deleting during Map iteration only affects entries not yet visited (future entries may be affected, but here we only delete the current key). The spread copies all keys into an array needlessly.

title: onChunk throttle check calls Date.now() for every received network chunk
description: Inside onChunk (the hot tee capture path), `const now = Date.now()` is evaluated on every incoming chunk to implement a 50ms progress throttle. Network streaming of a large JXL file can produce hundreds of chunks.
code_excerpt:
const now = Date.now();
        if (now - (job.lastProgressEmit || 0) > 50) {
evidence: Lines 597-600 confirmed: Date.now() is called on every chunk. For a streaming download at typical network speeds (e.g. 100 chunks/s at 4KB each), this is 100 syscalls/s for a throttle that only fires every 50ms.

title: concatUint8Arrays filters with instanceof checks on every element even when called with a known-valid array
description: concatUint8Arrays begins with `.filter((c): c is Uint8Array => c instanceof Uint8Array && c.byteLength > 0)`, performing a typeof/instanceof test on every element. This function is only called from within the package with arrays of Uint8Arrays (no mixed types).
code_excerpt: const valid = chunks.filter((c): c is Uint8Array => c instanceof Uint8Array && c.byteLength > 0);
evidence: Line 106 confirmed: instanceof filter + intermediate array allocation for internally-controlled inputs. Minor but confirmed.

title: selectTiers allocates a filtered array (before70) even when the result would be the same element as dcEvent
description: selectTiers calls `events.filter(e => e.byteOffset < totalBytes * 0.7)` and takes `before70[before70.length - 1]`. This allocates a filtered array of potentially all events just to get the last qualifying element.
evidence: Lines 78-80 confirmed: filter allocates a new array to find the last qualifying element. A reverse scan would find it in O(n) with no allocation. Minor but confirmed.

title: No benchmark or regression test for scheduler throughput under maxQueuedJobs load
description: There are no benchmarks measuring tick() time with a full 50-job queue, onChunk throughput under simulated streaming, or armEarliestRetryTimer cost. Without a perf baseline, the per-RAF allocation patterns (performance-1, performance-2, performance-3) can silently regress.
code_excerpt: private tick(): void {
evidence: No benchmark files found under packages/jxl-progressive/. Confirmed performance-1/2/3 are real allocations in the hot path with no regression protection. Capability genuinely missing.
